project.title: Bacterial stigmasterol degradation involving radical flavin delta-24 desaturase and molybdenum-dependent C26 hydroxylase
project.projectDescription: Sterols are ubiquitous membrane constituents that persist to a large extent in the environment due to their water insolubility and chemical inertness. Recently, an oxygenase-independent sterol degradation pathway was discovered in a cholesterol-grown denitrifying bacterium Sterolibacterium (S.) denitrificans. It achieves hydroxylation of the unactivated primary C26 of the isoprenoid side chain to an allylic alcohol via a phosphorylated intermediate in a four-step ATP-dependent enzyme cascade. However, this pathway is incompatible with the degradation of widely distributed steroids containing a double bond at C-22 in the isoprenoid side chain such as the plant sterol stigmasterol. Here, we have enriched a prototypical delta-24 desaturase from S. denitrificans, which catalyses the electron acceptor-dependent oxidation of the intermediate stigmast-1,4-diene-3-one (SDO) to a conjugated 22,(E)24-diene. We suggest an 44 architecture of the 440 kDa enzyme, with each subunit covalently binding an FMN cofactor to a histidyl residue. As isolated, both flavins are present as red semiquinone radicals, which can be reduced by SDO but cannot be oxidised even with strong oxidising agents. We propose a mechanism involving an allylic radical intermediate in which two flavin semiquinones each abstract one hydrogen atom from the substrate. The conjugated delta-22,24 moiety formed allows for the subsequent hydroxylation of the terminal C26 with water by a heterologously produced molybdenum-dependent steroid C26 dehydrogenase. In conclusion, the pathway elucidated for delta-22 steroids achieves oxygen-independent hydroxylation of the isoprenoid side chain by bypassing the ATP-dependent formation of a phosphorylated intermediate.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: Samples for liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) analyses were separated by SDS-PAGE. Following visualization of proteins with colloidal Coomassie Blue, gel bands were excised and proteins were in-gel digested using trypsin for subsequent MS analysis essentially as described (Landwehr et al., 2021, https://doi.org/10.3389/fmolb.2021.643696). In brief, peptides mixtures were separated on an Ultimate 3000 RSLCnano coupled to a Q-Exactive Plus mass spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Dreieich, Germany). Peptides were washed and concentrated on µPAC™ trapping columns and analysed on a 50 cm µPAC™ analytical column (both Thermo Fisher Scientific, Dreieich, Germany) using a 75 min gradient of solvent A (0.1% FA) and solvent B (86% ACN; 0.1% FA). Data dependent acquisition consisted of full MS scans in the range of m/z 370–1,700; resolution of 70,000 at m/z 400; and fragmentation of the 15 most abundant multiply charged precursor ions by higher energy collisional dissociation (HCD) recorded with resolution of 35,000. Additionally, UV absorbance was monitored at a wavelength of 370nm, specific for FAD/FADH2.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: MS raw data were searched using MaxQuant (version 2.0.2.0) against the reference proteome protein sequences for S. denitrificans from UniProt (release 2022_03, taxonomy id 157592). Carbamidomethylation of cysteine residues was considered as fixed modification, oxidation of methionine, acetylation of protein N-termini as well as cofactors FMNH (monoisotopic mass of 454.0890) or FAD (783.1414) bound to histidine were considered as variable modifications. Proteins were identified with at least one unique peptide and a false discovery rate of 0.01 on both peptide and protein level.
